elements.sent-2411-001: The states accepted by a quantum circuit are known as the witnesses for the quantum circuit's satisfiability.
elements.sent-2411-002: The assumption $\BQP \neq \QMA$ implies that no efficient algorithm exists for constructing a witness for a quantum circuit from the circuit's classical description.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, a similar complexity-theoretic lower bound on the computational hardness of cloning a witness is not known.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this note, we derive a conjecture about cloning algorithms for maximally entangled states over hidden subspaces which would imply that no efficient algorithm exists for cloning witnesses (assuming $\BQP \not \supseteq \NP$).
elements.sent-2411-005: The conjecture and result follow from connections between quantum computation and representation theory; specifically, the relationship between quantum state complexity and the complexity of computing Kronecker coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-006: We say that a transformation from $n$-qubits to $2n$-qubits clones a quantum state $\ket{\psi \in \qubits{n}$ if $\ket{\psi \mapsto \ket{\psi \otimes \ket{\psi}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The no-cloning theorem proves that there is no single transformation for cloning a pair of non-orthogonal quantum states due to the linearity of quantum mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-008: For example, there is no linear transformation that simultaneously maps: \begin{equation}\ket{0} \mapsto \ket{0} \otimes \ket{0} \quad \text{and} \quad \ket{+} \mapsto \ket{+} \otimes \ket{+}, \text{ where: } \ket{+} \defeq \frac{\ket{0} + \ket{1}}{\sqrt{2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this note, we focus on the computational hardness of cloning and do not consider information-theoretic issues with it.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is because we study the complexity of witness cloning, the task of efficiently preparing the state $\ket{\psi \otimes \ket{\psi}$ given as input a single copy of $\ket{\psi}$ and the classical description of a $\poly(n)$-sized quantum circuit $V$ that uniquely accepts $\ket{\psi}$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: With access to the verifier $V$, the task is relieved of its information-theoretic barriers because a computationally unbounded algorithm exists for deriving the second copy of the state $\ket{\psi}$ from the description of $V$ without touching the initial copy.
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, the computational task of witness cloning is necessarily as easy as the task of constructing witnesses.
elements.sent-2411-013: The goal of this paper is to provide evidence that the task of cloning is not significantly easier than the aforementioned tasks -- i.e. the task of witness cloning does not have a polynomial-time quantum algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-014: The definition can be generalized to verifiers $V$ which accept multiple orthogonal states.
elements.sent-2411-015: In general, we say a transformation clones the witnesses of $V$ if any witness of $V$ is mapped to a $2n$-qubit state such that the reduced density matrices on the first $n$ qubits and the last $n$ qubits would be individually accepted by $V$.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is because we regard the witness as a ``witness to the satisfiability of $V$''.
elements.sent-2411-017: In other words, if $\Ll$ is the subspace of witnesses of $V$, the transformation maps $\Ll$ to $\Ll \otimes \Ll$.
elements.sent-2411-018: It is important to note that for verifiers $V$ which accept multiple orthogonal states, the transformation may produce entangled states $\in \Ll \otimes \Ll$ due to linearity.
elements.sent-2411-019: One technique for proving a lower-bound is showing that a black-box oracle for witness cloning implies an unexpected complexity class collapse\footnote{Since the cloning task is not a decision problem, we cannot directly connect the complexity of this task to a decision complexity class.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Equivalently, given access to a quantum oracle for cloning witnesses, there exists a polynomial-time quantum algorithm for deciding any $\QMA$ problem.
elements.sent-2411-021: We do not know such a statement and it is plausible that there exists an efficient witness cloning algorithm for all verification circuits.
elements.sent-2411-022: If an efficient witness cloning algorithm were to exist, it would imply that the complexity of constructing multiple copies of a witness is entirely concentrated in the complexity of constructing the first copy.
elements.sent-2411-023: Despite not knowing complexity-theoretic evidence, we do know oracle separations proving the computational hardness of witness cloning~\cite{aaronson-christiano as well as hardness results based on cryptographic primitives~\cite{quantum-money-from-knots,broadbent2023uncloneablequantumadvice,zhandry-quantum-lightning,zhandry-group-actions,bostanci2024generalquantumdualityrepresentations .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Most of these are studied in the context of public-key quantum money schemes, which can be thought of as an average-case hardness analog of the worst-case hardness results we study in this work.
elements.sent-2411-026: Aaronson and Christiano~\cite{aaronson-christiano consider an oracle defined as the indicator function for $(\{0\} \times A) \sqcup (\{1\} \times A^\perp)$ where $A \subset \FF_2^n$ is a linear subspace of dimension $n/2$ and $A^\perp$ is the space of vectors orthogonal to it.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Given access to such an oracle, there exists an efficient algorithm which only accepts the subset state $\ket{A}$ by measuring the input in the standard and Hadamard bases and comparing to the oracles for $A$ and $A^\perp$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, they prove, using an adaptation of the adversary/hybrid method, that any algorithm cloning the state $\ket{A}$ for all such linear subspaces $A$ must make an exponential number of queries to the oracle, thereby proving a rigorous exponential lower bound on the task of cloning.
elements.sent-2411-029: Zhandry~\cite{zhandry-quantum-lightning shows that the oracle $(\{0\} \times A) \sqcup (\{1\} \times A^\perp)$ can be suitably hidden assuming quantum indistinguishability obfuscation in such a way that one can prove hardness of the cloning task without the oracle but requires a purported and non-standard cryptographic assumption (that is yet to be broken).}
elements.sent-2411-030: In~\cite{zhandry-group-actions , Zhandry constructs a quantum money scheme based on abelian group actions.}
elements.sent-2411-031: He proved the security of the scheme in the generic group action (GGA) model, assuming a new but natural strengthening of the discrete log assumption on group actions.
elements.sent-2411-032: Since the discrete logarithm problem has a query-efficient algorithm \cite{ettinger04 , the GGA model cannot be used to give unconditional hardness results.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Zhandry's abelian group action scheme is an inspiration for the non-abelian group action results in this work.
elements.sent-2411-034: We also note a concurrent result by Bostanchi, Nehoran, and Zhandry~\cite{bostanci2024generalquantumdualityrepresentations generalizing Zhandry's work to non-abelian group actions for constructions of quantum money, lightning, and fire, cryptographic notions quantifying hardness-of-cloning.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We highly applaud this work and think that it is a big step towards understanding cryptographic hardness of these primitives.
elements.sent-2411-036: The specific goal of this work is to make progress on proving the hardness of cloning from a complexity-theoretic assumption such as $\BQP \not\supseteq \NP$.
elements.sent-2411-037: We make progress towards a complexity lower bound for witness cloning.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, we show that an efficient quantum algorithm for cloning witnesses implies $\BQP \supseteq \NP$, assuming a conjecture about cloning maximally entangled states over hidden subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-039: We believe that this conjecture (\Cref{conj:hidden-epr ) will prove to be significantly more tractable than the general problem.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We identify a family of $\NP$-hard maximally entangled states over hidden subspaces, defined shortly.
elements.sent-2411-041: This is due to a nascent connection between quantum computation and the complexity of Kronecker coefficients.

elements.sent-2411-043: al.~\cite{kronecker-coeff that Kronecker coefficients are a $\mathsf{\#BQP}$ quantity.}

elements.sent-2411-048: The unique state $\ket{\psi}$ such that $\ev{\Gamma_{\mu \nu \lambda {\psi = 1$ is the maximally entangled state over a hidden subspace.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For dimensions $d_1 \leq d_2$, and a subspace $\Pi \subseteq \CC^{d_2}$ of dimension $d_1$ and any basis $\ket*{b_1 , \ldots, \ket*{b_{d_1 }$ for $\Pi$, the corresponding maximally entangled witness state for the subspace $\Pi$ is \begin{equation}\ket*{\Phi_\Pi} \defeq \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_1}} \sum_{i = 1}^{d_1} \ket*{b_i} \otimes \ket*{b_i^*}. \label{eq:hidden-subspace}\end{equation} The state is independent of the choice of basis.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: If deciding if $\Pi \neq 0$ is $\NP$-hard, then we call the subspace hidden.
elements.sent-2411-051: This is because there is no quantum polynomial-time algorithm for generating states in $\Pi$ assuming $\BQP \not \supseteq \NP$.
elements.sent-2411-052: This produces our main result: if witness cloning is efficient for all verifiers, then the cloner must clone the maximally entangled states corresponding to a family of $\NP$-hard hidden subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-053: We believe that the maximally entangled nature of the witness prohibits the existence of an efficient cloner.
elements.sent-2411-054: We pose a conjecture~(\Cref{conj:hidden-epr ) which asserts that if an efficient cloner for the maximally entangled state over hidden subspace $\Pi$ exists, then there exists an efficient circuit generating a state in $\Pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Together, this conjecture and $\BQP \not \supseteq \NP$ imply that witness cloning is hard.
elements.sent-2411-056: We believe~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr is a significant step towards lower bounding the complexity of witness cloning.}

elements.sent-2411-059: We call $\Ll$ the space of accepting states by $V$ and $\Ll^\perp$ the space of rejecting states.
elements.sent-2411-060: We say that a quantum transformation clones the groundspace of $\H$ if it maps any groundstate of $\H$ (an eigenvector of minimal eigenvalue) to a groundstate of the Hamiltonian $\H \otimes \II + \II \otimes \H$ which describes a $2n$-qubit system.
elements.sent-2411-061: By standard arguments about Hamiltonian energy estimation, we note that any local Hamiltonian $0 \preccurlyeq \H \preccurlyeq \II $ can be transformed into a verification circuit with the property that $c - s \geq \gamma$, the spectral gap of the local Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-062: The computational task of cloning is to construct two accepting states from one accepting state $\ket{\psi}$ and the description of a verification circuit $V$.
elements.sent-2411-063: We will call this witness cloning for $V$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We believe that there is no efficient transformation for cloning all verification circuits $V$ for completeness $2/3$ and soundness $1/3$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $G$ be a finite group and let $\Ii_G$ be the set of distinct irreducible representations -- i.e. irreps -- for $G$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Notationally, $\lambda \in \Ii_G$ will parameterize the irreps of $G$ with $\fn{\rho^\lambda {G {\CC^{d_\lambda \times d_\lambda }$ being the corresponding irrep of dimension $d_\lambda$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Let $\chi^\rho(\cdot) = \tr(\rho(\cdot))$ be the character of a representation $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-068: When $\rho = \rho^\lambda$ is an irrep, for brevity, we use the notation $\chi^\lambda$ to refer to $\chi^{\rho^\lambda}$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Two important (reducible) representations are the left- and right-regular representations, defined by their action on a $|G|$-dimensional vector space with orthonormal basis $\lbrace \ket{g \rbrace_{g\in G}$: \begin{equation}\rho_L(g) = \sum_{h \in H} \ketbra{gh}{h}, \qquad \rho_R(g) = \sum_{h \in H} \ketbra*{hg}{h}, \qquad \rho_C = \rho_L \rho_R = \rho_R \rho_L. \label{eq:lr-reg-reps}\end{equation} Every representation $\sigma$ of a finite group has a unique decomposition irreps that is defined by the multiplicity coefficients $m_{\sigma \lambda}$: \begin{equation}\sigma \cong \bigoplus_{\lambda \in \Ii_G} \II_{m_{\sigma \lambda}} \otimes \rho^\lambda.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-070: It is a standard fact that the left- and right-regular representations have decompositions with multiplicity coefficients $m_{\rho \lambda = d_\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The unitary transformation that decomposes both left- and right regular-representations is the Fourier transform.
elements.sent-2411-073: For any finite group $G$, the Fourier transform unitary is given by \begin{equation}\mathrm{FT} \defeq \sum_{\pi \in G} \sum_{\lambda \in \Ii_G} \sum_{i,j=1}^{d_\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{d_\lambda}{\abs{G}}} \rho^{\lambda}_{ij}(\pi) \ket{\lambda, i, j}\bra{\pi}. \label{eq:qft}\end{equation} Here $\ket{\lambda}$ is some classical representation of the irrep; in the case of the symmetric group, the irreps can be represented by Young tableaus.
elements.sent-2411-074: For certain groups, including the symmetric group, application of the Fourier transform is efficient~ \cite{beals97, moore2003genericquantumfouriertransforms .}
elements.sent-2411-075: We describe all the transformations in this note with a finite group $G$, but our attention will eventually focus on $G = S_n$, the symmetric group --- i.e., the group of permutations on $n$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-076: For the symmetric group, the set of irreps, $\Ii_{S_n}$, can be parametrized by partitions $\lambda \vdash n$ or equivalent Young tableau of $n$ boxes.
elements.sent-2411-077: When the group is $S_n$ and the representation being studied $\sigma = \rho^\mu \otimes \rho^\nu$, then the coefficients are abbreviated as $m_{\mu \nu \lambda}$ and are known as Kronecker coefficients.

elements.sent-2411-144: Then, we conjecture that there exists an efficient polynomial-time algorithm which takes as input the classical description $\langle V \rangle$ and produces a state in the support of $\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-145: One way to view~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr is that it posits that any white-box circuit for the cloner of $\ket*{\Phi_{\Pi }$ must leak the description of a circuit which creates a state in the support of $\Pi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: The direct application of~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr is the following hardness-of-cloning result:.}

elements.sent-2411-148: Assume there exists an algorithm for cloning the states passing the $(\mu \otimes \nu, \lambda)$-verification algorithm when $m_{\mu \nu \lambda = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-149: By~\Cref{cor:passing-all-three-tests , we know that said verification algorithm is a verification circuit (\Cref{def:verification-ckt ) for completeness $c = 1$ and soundness $s \leq 8/9$ with a unique state passing the verification circuit.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Furthermore, by the previous argument, said state is a hidden maximally entangled state for hidden subspace $\Pi$~\eqref{eq:U-partial-isometry-hidden-EPR .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Therefore,~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr directly implies that there exists an efficient circuit for constructing a state in the image of $\Xi_\lambda = \Xi_\lambda^{(\mu \nu)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-152: So, running this algorithm produces a quantum certificate that $\dim \Xi_\lambda = m_{\mu \nu \lambda d_\lambda \neq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-153: As the certificate is efficiently verifiable due to Weak Fourier Sampling (\Cref{fact:weak-fourier-sampling ), we have a $\BQP$ algorithm for solving $\mathsf{UNIQUE-NP}$.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Application of the randomized reduction of Valiant and Vazirani~\cite{VALIANT198685 yields $\BQP \supseteq \NP$.}
elements.subsection-2411-007: The plausibility of the cloning hidden maximally entangled states conjecture
elements.sent-2411-155: Our attempt at proving the hardness of witness cloning from complexity-theoretic assumptions harks on~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr .}
elements.sent-2411-156: For this reason, we believe that this is an ``unfinished work'' and we are hopeful that it will spur interest in the community towards this problem.
elements.paragraph-2411-005: The difficulty in cloning a hidden maximally entangled state state
elements.sent-2411-157: We made many attempts at constructing a circuit for cloning a hidden maximally entangled state $\ket*{\Phi_\Pi}$ that did not immediately reveal a circuit for generating a state within $\Pi$ (in an attempt to prove~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr false).}
elements.sent-2411-159: Roughly speaking, if the first copy of the state $\ket*{\Phi_\Pi}$ is supported on registers $A_1A_2$ and we intend to clone the state into $B_1B_2$, there is no transformation we can run on $A_1B_1$ and on $A_2B_2$ to clone the state as the entanglement between $A_1B_1$ and $A_2B_2$ will persist.
elements.sent-2411-160: Morally, it appears that cloning the state requires disentangling the original state across $A_1A_2$.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, we believe that such a disentangler run backwards would generate a state in $\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-162: It follows that a cloning circuit that does not reveal a generating circuit must efficiently rotate the standard basis vectors into an orthonormal set of vectors that includes $\ket*{\Phi_\Pi}$ without constructing a state for generating states in $\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-163: However, we emphasize that these statements are only intuitions and we were unable to make any of them rigorous.
elements.paragraph-2411-006: A white-box proof is necessary for~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr}
elements.sent-2411-164: It is important to note that ~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr cannot follow a black-box reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-165: The ideal resolution of~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr would be in the black-box setting where one would show that a black-box (or oracle) gate for cloning the hidden maximally entangled state can be used $O(\poly(n))$ times as a subroutine in an algorithm constructing a state in $\Pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-166: However, we know that this is not true due to the observations of Nehoran and Zhandry~\cite{nehoran_et_al:LIPIcs.ITCS.2024.82 .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Nehoran and Zhandry show the existence of a quantum oracle model in which cloning of verifiable states is easy and yet construction of said verifiable states is hard.
elements.sent-2411-168: Therefore, any proof of~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr must be quantum non-relativizing.}
elements.sent-2411-169: At a high level, the Nehoran and Zhandry~\cite{nehoran_et_al:LIPIcs.ITCS.2024.82 intuition when applied to~\Cref{conj:hidden-epr is to consider a quantum oracle which performs the 2-dimensional reflection which reflects $\ket{\Phi_\Pi ^{\otimes 2}$ onto $\ket{\Phi_\Pi}\ket{0}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: The action of the oracle in the space orthogonal to this 2-dimensional subspace is identity.
elements.sent-2411-171: Equivalently, with access to this quantum oracle cloning is easy and yet this oracle cannot be used to create a state within $\Pi$.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Basing the argument on average-case vs worst-case assumptions
elements.sent-2411-172: In our research of this problem, we came across the observation that it is appears significantly easier to prove hardness-of-cloning statements from average-case assumptions rather than worst-case assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-173: Ideally, one would like to show that having access to an oracle for witness cloning would drastically improve the power of $\BQP$ (to include say $\NP$ or $\QMA$).
elements.sent-2411-174: However, it isn't clear how to use the oracle effectively as the only states a polynomial-time algorithm could query the oracle are already are efficiently constructable; therefore.
elements.sent-2411-175: In essence, this becomes a chicken-and-egg problem: a cloner is only effective for generating hard states given a hard state.
elements.sent-2411-176: Instead, armed with an average-case assumption, we can create a random ensemble over states which are each individually verifiable and yet hard to construct on average.
elements.sent-2411-177: Crucially, the ensemble must be superpolynomial in size.
elements.sent-2411-178: This is the basis of quantum lightning schemes.
elements.sent-2411-179: In lightning schemes, the average-case hardness assumption is used to argue that an efficient cloner for most states in the ensemble cannot exist.
elements.sent-2411-180: Notice that a worst-case assumption would not suffice as it may only apply to a negligible fraction of the states in the ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-181: Since we do not know of average-case hardness statements for any $\NP$-complete problems, proving the hardness-of-cloning from an assumption like $\BQP \not \supseteq \NP$ appears to require fundamentally different techniques.
elements.sent-2411-182: One of the appealing properties of Kronecker coefficients which inspired our investigation is that the average-case complexity of computing Kronecker coefficients is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-183: If we were able to prove an average-case statement for computing Kronecker coefficients, then Kronecker coefficient may lead to a new quantum lightning construction.

elements.sent-2411-186: We want to thank Sergey Bravyi for many fruitful discussions.
elements.sent-2411-187: Most of the representation theory results used in this work stem from Schur's lemma: \begin{fact [Schur's lemma] For any $1 \leq i_1, j_1 \leq d_{\lambda_1}$ and $1 \leq i_2, j_2, \leq d_{\lambda_2}$ for irreps $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $G$, \begin{equation}\sum_{g \in G} \rho^{\lambda_1}_{i_1j_1}(g)^* \rho^{\lambda_2}_{i_2 j_2}(g) = \frac{\abs{G}}{d_{\lambda_1}} \cdot \delta_{\lambda_1 \lambda_2} \delta_{i_1 i_2} \delta_{j_1 j_2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-188: It follows by taking the sum over all $i_1 = j_1$ and $i_2 = j_2$.
elements.sent-2411-190: For any $d \times d$ matrix $A$, let us define $\ket{\mathrm{vec \ A \defeq \sum_{i,j = 1 ^d A_{ij \ket{i}\ket{j}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Then $\norm{\ket{\mathrm{vec \ A = \norm{A _F$, the Frobenius norm of the matrix.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Additionally, the inner product between two vectorizations is equal to \begin{equation}\bra{\mathrm{vec} \ A} \ket{\mathrm{vec} \ B} = \sum_{i,j = 1}^d A_{ij}^* B_{ij} = \sum_{ij}^d A^\dagger_{ji} B_{ij} = \tr(A^\dagger B) = \ev{A, B}_F.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\bra{vec \ A} \ket{vec \ B} = \sum_{i,j = 1}^d A_{ij}^* B_{ij} = \sum_{ij}^d A^\dagger_{ji} B_{ij} = \tr(A^\dagger B) = \ev{A, B}_F.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-193: And for $B, C \in \CC^{d \times d}$, $B \otimes C \ket{\mathrm{vec}\ A = \ket*{\mathrm{vec}\ BAC^\top}$.}
elements.sent-2411-194: This shows that $\lbrace \Xi_\lambda \rbrace_{\lambda \in \Ii_G }$ is a set of orthogonal projectors.
elements.sent-2411-196: By orthogonality and the trace equation, it follows that $\sum_{\lambda \in \Ii_G \Xi_\lambda = \II_D$, and therefore this is a POVM over $D$ dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-197: This measurement can be implemented efficiently whenever the quantum $G$-Fourier transform can be implemented efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-198: We use Harrow's generalized phase estimation algorithm\cite{harrow2005applicationscoherentclassicalcommunication .}
elements.sent-2411-199: This circuit uses two registers, a control and a target.
elements.sent-2411-200: The control register is initialized as thee uniform superposition over $G$, and the controlled action of $\rho$ is applied to the target register.
elements.sent-2411-201: The irrep label is then measured in the Fourier basis to conclude the computation: \begin{equation}\qquad \qquad \Qcircuit @C=1em @R=1.5em { \lstick{\frac{1}{\sqrt{G}} \sum_{g \in G} \ket{g}} {/} \qw \ctrl{1} \gate{FT^\dagger} \meter \cw \lambda \lstick{\ket{\phi}} {/} \qw \gate{\rho} \qw \qw \Xi_\lambda \ket{\psi} }\end{equation} Similarly to~\cite[Theorem 1]{larocca2024quantumalgorithmsrepresentationtheoreticmultiplicities , we can characterize the action of this circuit using a superoperator $E_\lambda^\dag (\cdot) E_\lambda$ with elements $E_\lambda$: \begin{equation}E_\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{|G|}} \sum_{g \in G} \left( \Pi_\lambda FT\ket{g} \right)_C \otimes \rho(g)_T,\end{equation} where $\Pi_\lambda$ measures the irrep label in the Fourier basis: \begin{equation}\Pi_\lambda := \ketbra{\lambda} \otimes \II_{d_\lambda^2} = \sum_{i,j \in [d_\lambda]} \ket{\lambda,i,j} \bra{\lambda, i,j}\end{equation} and $\mathrm{FT}$ is the Fourier transform for $G$ previously defined in~\eqref{eq:qft .}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Therefore, \begin{equation}\Pi_\lambda FT \ket{g} = \sum_{i,j \in [d_\lambda]} \sqrt{\frac{d_\lambda}{|G|}} \rho^\lambda_{ij}(g) ^*\ket{\lambda, i, j}\end{equation} We now show that: $E_\lambda^\dag E_\lambda = \Xi_\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-203: We use the Young-Yamanouchi basis for $\rho^\mu$.
